title: Cigarettes russes
subtitle: Thin tuile biscuits rolled into cylinders while hot
excerpt: A French tuile biscuit baked into a thin, plain wafer and rolled around a thin dowel or pencil while still hot from the oven to set in a tight cylinder. Served with ice cream or as a petits fours.
sourceNotes: Cigarettes russes are a standard French confectionery form. The term appears in French patisserie guides from the late nineteenth century. Auguste Escoffier includes the rolled wafer form in Le Guide Culinaire (1903). They share the same batter base as almond tuiles but are made without nuts and rolled into cylinders rather than draped over a curved surface. The batter must be thin enough to set translucent when baked.

content.text: Cigarettes russes use the same batter as tuiles but are rolled tightly around a thin dowel, pencil, or the handle of a wooden spoon to form a hollow cylinder. The window between pliable and brittle is brief: 20-30 seconds from leaving the oven. Work quickly with a light touch. The cylinders can be left plain or dipped in chocolate after they cool.

Lay a thin dowel (or pencil) at the edge of the hot biscuit and roll the biscuit around it, pressing firmly as you go to create a tight cylinder. Hold in place for 10 seconds, then slide off and set seam-side down. If the biscuit tears, you've waited too long: return it to the oven for 30 seconds.

Heat the oven to 180°C (160°C fan). Whisk together the {{icing-sugar}}, {{egg-whites}}, and {{vanilla-extract}} until the sugar dissolves. Stir in the {{plain-flour}}, then stir in the cooled melted {{unsalted-butter}}. Drop teaspoons of batter onto a lined baking tray and spread each into a thin oval or circle about 8 cm across, using the back of a spoon. Space them 10 cm apart. Bake for 7-9 minutes until the edges are deep golden and the centres are light gold.

Remove the tray from the oven and work immediately. Slide a palette knife under each biscuit to loosen it. Place a thin dowel or the handle of a wooden spoon along one edge of the biscuit and roll the biscuit tightly around it, pressing to seal. Hold for 10 seconds, then slide off and set seam-side down on a wire rack. If a biscuit hardens before you can roll it, return to the oven for 30 seconds to soften.
